frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment: Sana beti, I understand you are upset, but you must think very carefully before taking this step. These days, 498A and domestic violence cases — they are being misused so much, the courts themselves are saying this. Your husband will be arrested immediately, his whole family, his aged parents — they will all be dragged to the police station, their reputation finished in the community, and then after two, three years the case goes nowhere because there was no real evidence. Is that what you want? We have seen so many cases here — a small argument, some misunderstanding, and the wife goes straight to police. Later the family is destroyed, the children suffer, and the girl herself regrets it. You are young, you have your whole life ahead. Let us sit together, call both families, we will resolve this in a proper Islamic way — with respect, with hikmah. The police and court — that is the last option, and honestly, for a matter like this, it should not even be the first thought.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes the 'legal system weaponisation' narrative by framing Sana's potential complaint as a misuse of law rather than a legitimate response to abuse, implicitly casting doubt on her credibility and centering the reputational harm to the husband's family over her physical safety. It is covert misogyny because it is delivered in a paternalistic, seemingly protective register that obscures victim-blaming and institutional gatekeeping of women's access to legal recourse.
persona_text: You are Mohammed. Age 44. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. You are a member of a community organisation that mediates family disputes. A case has come to you Ñ a young woman named Sana has come to the organisation saying her husband has been physically abusive and his family has been demanding more money from her parents. She wants help and is considering filing a police complaint. You are one of the mediators. You are in the mediation room with Sana and two other male mediators. You are responding to what Sana has said and advising her on what you think she should do and what filing a complaint will mean for her, her husband, and both families and what you believe about cases like hers.
